allergens: gluten (cornflour batter may contain wheat flour)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Veg Manchurian consists of approximately 6–8 deep-fried vegetable balls (about 200 g) served either dry or with a soy-chilli sauce coating.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Vegetable Preparations (Deep-fried mixed vegetable fritters / pakora analogue); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central cornflour batter and soy sauce component data
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Veg Manchurian in ICMR-NIN. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN deep-fried mixed vegetable preparations (pakora/mixed veg fritter profile) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for cornflour-battered deep-fried vegetables (NDB 11935 and related entries). Soy sauce sodium contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy soy sauce data. Energy and macros reflect a deep-fried cornflour-battered mixed vegetable ball with soy-chilli sauce coating.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — deep-fried battered vegetables / cornflour-battered fritter analogue
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Veg Manchurian. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food: cornflour-battered deep-fried vegetable fritters from the University of Sydney GI Database international tables. Cornflour has a GI of approximately 68–70 when consumed alone, but the presence of mixed vegetables, dietary fibre, fat from deep-frying, and protein moderates the overall GI of the composite dish to approximately 55 (low category). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 23.5 × 2.00) / 100 = 25.85, rounded to 25.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1040 mg per serving) from soy sauce and chilli sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Veg Manchurian due to its high sodium content from soy and chilli sauces; a single serving may contribute over 40–45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~55) and significant carbohydrate load (~47 g per serving) from cornflour batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Veg Manchurian in small portions due to its moderate GI and substantial carbohydrate content from the cornflour batter; pairing with high-fibre foods and monitoring post-meal blood glucose is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds significant fat (10.5 g per 100 g) and the dish is high in sodium from sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Veg Manchurian intake due to its elevated fat content from deep-frying and high sodium from soy and chilli sauces, both of which can adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Manchurian (also spelled Veg Munchurian) is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish widely served in Indian restaurants and street-food stalls across India, Singapore, and the Indian diaspora. It consists of finely chopped mixed vegetables — typically cabbage, carrot, green beans, and capsicum — bound with cornflour and all-purpose flour, shaped into small balls, and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The fried balls are then sautéed or tossed in a pungent, umami-rich sauce made from soy sauce, chilli sauce, ginger, garlic, and spring onions. The dish can be served dry (as a starter or snack) or with a thicker gravy (as a main course accompaniment to fried rice or noodles). The texture is crispy on the outside and soft inside, with a bold savoury, slightly spicy, and tangy flavour profile. Nutritionally, Veg Manchurian is energy-dense due to deep-frying (approximately 198 kcal per 100 g), with moderate carbohydrate content from the cornflour batter (~23.5 g per 100 g) and a meaningful fat contribution (~10.5 g per 100 g). The dish is notably high in sodium (~520 mg per 100 g) owing to soy and chilli sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low category), moderated by the fibre from mixed vegetables and the fat from deep-frying. Veg Manchurian is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly, but contains gluten (from cornflour/wheat flour batter) and soy allergens. It is not suitable for gluten-free diets.

What is the GI of Veg Manchurian?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Veg Manchurian exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN deep-fried mixed vegetable preparations and USDA FoodData Central cornflour-battered vegetable data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cornflour-battered deep-fried vegetable fritters as the closest proxy. Confidence is medium due to the adapted/estimated nature of both nutrient and GI data.
notes: 1. Spelling variant: the input record uses 'Veg Munchurian' (non-standard); the canonical English spelling is 'Veg Manchurian' — both forms are preserved in aliases. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; an exact laboratory analysis or a direct ICMR-NIN entry for Veg Manchurian would improve confidence to high. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: this dish is entirely plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods and vitamin D is naturally zero in this preparation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN vegetable preparation profiles. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 6. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (55 × 23.5 × 2.00) / 100 = 25.85, rounded to 25.8. 7. Sodium is high (~520 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce and chilli sauce; this is a primary clinical concern. 8. The dish is served at Mr Tandoor – Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 17.90.
